essential_question: Can you build a reference wall that sorts every angle rule you know into what's assumed and what's proven — and hold your own in a shuffle-and-match test?
objective: Build a six-card reference wall naming the Linear Pair Postulate, Vertical Angles Theorem, Corresponding Angles Postulate, Alternate Interior/Exterior Angles Theorems, and the Properties of Equality, each with its own diagram, then test recall with a shuffle-and-match round.

content.heading: From definitions to a permanent reference
bullets: Yesterday: defined postulate, theorem, and corollary, and named the specific rules behind vertical angles, linear pairs, and corresponding/alternate angles.
Today: build a six-card reference wall — one card per rule, each with its own small diagram — then test it with a shuffle-and-match round.
This wall becomes your 'reasons' column reference for the two-column proofs you write starting next unit.

content.heading: Gather + plan
content.note: Open the project card. Gather six index cards or cardstock rectangles, a ruler, and two marker colors — one for postulates, one for theorems. Decide where the finished wall will live (a corkboard, a taped strip on your wall, or a binder page) before you start drawing.

content.heading: One rule, one card, one diagram
bullets: Card 1 — Linear Pair Postulate: statement + a small diagram of two adjacent angles forming a straight line. Postulate color.
Card 2 — Vertical Angles Theorem: statement + an X diagram with the vertical pair arc-marked, plus the note 'proven from the Linear Pair Postulate.' Theorem color.
Card 3 — Corresponding Angles Postulate: statement + a parallel-lines-and-transversal diagram with one corresponding pair marked. Postulate color.

Card 4 — Alternate Interior Angles Theorem: statement + the same diagram with one alternate interior pair marked, plus the proof chain noted (Vertical Angles Theorem + Corresponding Angles Postulate). Theorem color.
Card 5 — Alternate Exterior Angles Theorem: statement + one alternate exterior pair marked, same proof-chain note. Theorem color.
Card 6 — Properties of Equality/Congruence: one card, three rows — Reflexive (A≅A), Symmetric (A≅B → B≅A), Transitive (A≅B and B≅C → A≅C).
Mount all six on your wall or board, postulates and theorems visibly color-coded, so you can tell assumed from proven at a glance.

content.preview: Session 3: argue why the Vertical Angles Theorem must be a theorem, not a postulate; review MUS Lesson Practice 22A/22B; take the checkpoint.

success_criteria.text: I can build six clearly labeled reference cards for the Linear Pair Postulate, Vertical Angles Theorem, Corresponding Angles Postulate, Alternate Interior/Exterior Angles Theorems, and the Properties of Equality
I can sort my own cards into postulate (assumed) versus theorem (proven) at a glance, by color
I can win a shuffle-and-match round pairing each angle-relationship statement to the specific postulate or theorem that names it

questions.prompt: List the six cards you built, in order, and mark each one P (postulate) or T (theorem) — or 'reference' for the properties card.
questions.answer: 1. Linear Pair Postulate (P). 2. Vertical Angles Theorem (T). 3. Corresponding Angles Postulate (P). 4. Alternate Interior Angles Theorem (T). 5. Alternate Exterior Angles Theorem (T). 6. Properties of Equality/Congruence (reference card — reflexive, symmetric, transitive).
questions.explain: Only two of the six are postulates; three are theorems built from them, and the sixth is a reference tool, not a claim about angles at all.

questions.prompt: On the grid, sketch the diagram you drew for your Vertical Angles Theorem card — two lines crossing at a point with the vertical pair arc-marked.
questions.answer: Two lines crossing at a point with the two vertical angles marked with matching arcs, labeled congruent.
questions.explain: Rubric: a clearly crossing pair of lines (1 pt) + the correct vertical pair marked with matching arc marks (1 pt).

questions.prompt: Shuffle your six statement cards away from your six name/reason cards, then re-match them. Record how many tries it took (or your time), and name the ONE pair that tricked you most — which two rules are easiest to mix up, and why?
questions.answer: Answers vary; a common trick pair is the Alternate Interior vs. Alternate Exterior Angles Theorem, since both require 'opposite sides of the transversal' — the only difference is interior vs. exterior position.
questions.explain: Rubric: an honest count/time recorded (1 pt) + a specific, correctly reasoned trick pair named (1 pt).

questions.prompt: Reflect: which is harder to keep straight — telling a postulate from a theorem, or telling the different NAMES of the angle theorems apart? What's your trick for remembering which is which?
